title: About
text: I am a results-driven data professional with six years of experience in the IT sector, currently refining my expertise through a Master’s in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. My background includes a strong foundation in ETL development and data visualization, with a proven track record of delivering technical solutions at global organizations like Cognizant and Infosys. I am particularly interested in the strategic application of machine learning and data science to drive innovation in healthcare and social entrepreneurship.

title: Sales Trend Analysis
text: This project analyses simulated monthly sales data to identify trends and visualise performance across product categories.

title: Sales Trend Analysis
text: This project analyses simulated monthly sales data to identify trends and visualise performance across product categories.

title: Sales Trend Analysis
text:
Libraries Used



Library
Purpose




pandas
Data manipulation


numpy
Generating sample data


plotly.express
Interactive visualisations

title: Sales Trend Analysis
text:
Analysis

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

# Generate sample sales data
np.random.seed(42)
months = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", periods=12, freq="ME")
categories = ["Electronics", "Clothing", "Food"]

data = []
for cat in categories:
    sales = np.random.randint(5000, 20000, size=12)
    for month, sale in zip(months, sales):
        data.append({"Month": month, "Category": cat, "Sales": sale})

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Line chart
fig = px.line(
    df,
    x="Month",
    y="Sales",
    color="Category",
    title="Monthly Sales by Category (2024)",
    markers=True
)
fig.show()

title: Sales Trend Analysis
text:
Key Findings






Tip



Electronics showed the highest variance month-to-month, suggesting seasonal demand patterns worth investigating further.



Electronics showed the highest sales overall.
Food remained the most consistent category throughout the year.
Clothing peaked mid-year, likely due to seasonal demand.

title: Reflection
text:
Throughout this module I developed skills across several areas:

Git & Version Control — Learning to commit incrementally and write descriptive messages made tracking changes much easier.
Quarto — Building a full website with Quarto taught me how to combine code, narrative, and design in one place.
Python — I strengthened my data analysis and visualisation skills using pandas and Plotly.
Web Design — I learned how SCSS, themes, and layout work together to create a professional site.

title: Reflection
text:
Data Analytics in Practice
A key concept in analytics is the linear regression formula:
\[y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon\]
Where \(y\) is the predicted outcome, \(x\) is the input variable, and \(\epsilon\) represents the error term.[^2]

title: Reflection
text:
Challenges






Warning



The most challenging part was understanding YAML indentation — small formatting errors can break the entire page.


A second challenge was integrating active Python code chunks into Quarto pages. Coming from a background in ETL development, I was familiar with Python but had to learn how Quarto handles code execution, output rendering, and interactive Plotly visualisations within a static site context.

title: Reflection
text:
Connecting to My Future






Tip



These skills are directly relevant to roles in data analytics, where communicating findings clearly is just as important as the analysis itself.


This module has been a turning point in how I think about data work. Having spent six years in data engineering at Cognizant and Infosys, I have strong technical foundations — but this portfolio has shown me the power of combining data science, analytics, and clear communication into one cohesive skill set.
My goal is to bring together my experience in data engineering, data science, and business analytics to deliver end-to-end value for organisations — from raw data pipelines all the way to strategic business insights. I believe this combination is what makes a truly impactful data professional in today’s industry.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis — Gapminder Dataset
text:
In this project I explored the Gapminder dataset to understand relationships between GDP, life expectancy, and population across countries and years.



Global development trends visualised using the Gapminder dataset

title: Exploratory Data Analysis — Gapminder Dataset
text:
In this project I explored the Gapminder dataset to understand relationships between GDP, life expectancy, and population across countries and years.



Global development trends visualised using the Gapminder dataset

title: Exploratory Data Analysis — Gapminder Dataset
text:
Libraries Used



Library
Purpose




pandas
Data manipulation


plotly.express
Interactive visualisations

title: Exploratory Data Analysis — Gapminder Dataset
text:
Analysis

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

# Load built-in Gapminder dataset
df = px.data.gapminder()

# Filter for most recent year
df_2007 = df[df['year'] == 2007]

# Interactive scatter plot
fig = px.scatter(
    df_2007,
    x="gdpPercap",
    y="lifeExp",
    size="pop",
    color="continent",
    hover_name="country",
    log_x=True,
    title="GDP per Capita vs Life Expectancy (2007)",
    labels={"gdpPercap": "GDP per Capita", "lifeExp": "Life Expectancy"}
)
fig.show()

title: Exploratory Data Analysis — Gapminder Dataset
text:
Key Findings






Note



Countries with higher GDP per capita generally show higher life expectancy, though with diminishing returns at very high income levels.



Europe and Oceania consistently show the highest life expectancy.
Africa shows the most variation, suggesting other factors at play.

text:
